text.title: Safe Refactoring Workflow
text.summary: The micro-edit loop: ensure coverage, make one change, run tests, commit, repeat.

rule.statement: Ensure test coverage exists before refactoring. Write characterization tests for uncovered branches first.
rule.rationale: Without tests, refactoring has no safety net. Characterization tests capture current behavior (even if wrong) so you know if you changed it.

rule.statement: Apply one named refactor per commit. Commit message names the pattern: "refactor: extract method validate_order".
rule.rationale: Granular commits are reviewable and revertable. Mixed patterns in one commit make review and rollback intractable.

rule.statement: Run tests after every micro-edit. If tests fail, revert the change — do not continue refactoring on a red baseline.
rule.rationale: Accumulating failures hides which change broke what. Always refactor on a green baseline.

rule.statement: Use IDE/LSP refactoring tools (Extract Method, Rename, Move) over manual edits. They maintain references automatically.
rule.rationale: Manual edits miss string-based references (JSON keys, DB column names, reflection). IDE tools update all references in a single operation.

rule.statement: For large-scale refactors, introduce the new shape alongside the old, redirect callers one by one, then delete the old. Never big-bang rewrite.
rule.rationale: Parallel coexistence means the system is always deployable. Big-bang rewrites have a long broken period and high integration risk.
